steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis (given pancreatic stone with post-ductal dilation on prior CT), mesenteric ischemia (given mechanical valves with subtherapeutic INR 1.9, splenic infarction, and right middle finger necrosis suggesting embolic/thrombotic disease), bowel obstruction (history of recurrent SBOs with ostomy)
ensemble.information_gap: The prior CT abdomen (without contrast, or non-diagnostic for vascular detail) showed a pancreatic stone with post-ductal dilation and splenic infarction but could not adequately characterize pancreatic parenchymal necrosis/severity, biliary ductal anatomy, mesenteric vascular perfusion, or subtle bowel ischemia — all of which require IV contrast for delineation.
ensemble.expected_finding: Enhancement pattern of the pancreatic parenchyma to assess for necrotizing pancreatitis; biliary ductal dilation or choledocholithiasis explaining the pancreatic stone and elevated lipase; and mesenteric vessel patency (SMA/SMV) to evaluate for ischemia given the embolic risk profile (mechanical valves, subtherapeutic INR, splenic infarction, digital necrosis).
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With a lipase of 2017 IU/L (>33× ULN), a pancreatic stone with post-ductal dilation on prior imaging, and high embolic risk from subtherapeutic anticoagulation with mechanical valves and evidence of end-organ ischemia (splenic infarction, digital necrosis), contrast-enhanced CT is the appropriate next step to grade pancreatitis severity, identify biliary obstruction, and evaluate for concurrent mesenteric ischemia.
grounding: History: CT abdomen showed no acute intra-abdominal process, unchanged splenic infarction, pancreatic stone with post ductal dilation
History: 2 week history of hypoperfusion to right middle finger, resulting of necrosis distal to the DIP
History: INR was most recently low at 1.9, with adjustment in his coumadin dose
History: 2 mechanical valves (prosthetic aortic and mitral valves)
History: recurrent small bowel obstructions with an ostomy in place
History: ESRD on HD, CAD, Diastolic CHF
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a massively elevated lipase (2017 IU/L) with a pancreatic stone and post-ductal dilation on the prior non-contrast CT, making pancreatitis the leading diagnosis, but the severity — specifically whether there is pancreatic necrosis — cannot be determined without IV contrast. Simultaneously, the constellation of subtherapeutic INR on mechanical valves, splenic infarction, and digital necrosis raises serious concern for ongoing thromboembolic disease that could involve the mesenteric vasculature, which would be life-threatening and requires contrast-enhanced imaging to evaluate. A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is therefore ordered to simultaneously grade pancreatitis severity, assess the biliary tree, and evaluate mesenteric vessel patency.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis (given pancreatic stone with post-ductal dilation on prior CT), mesenteric ischemia (given mechanical valves with subtherapeutic INR 1.9, splenic infarction, and right middle finger necrosis suggesting embolic/thrombotic disease), bowel obstruction (history of recurrent SBOs with ostomy)
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The prior CT abdomen (without contrast, or non-diagnostic for vascular detail) showed a pancreatic stone with post-ductal dilation and splenic infarction but could not adequately characterize pancreatic parenchymal necrosis/severity, biliary ductal anatomy, mesenteric vascular perfusion, or subtle bowel ischemia — all of which require IV contrast for delineation.
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Enhancement pattern of the pancreatic parenchyma to assess for necrotizing pancreatitis; biliary ductal dilation or choledocholithiasis explaining the pancreatic stone and elevated lipase; and mesenteric vessel patency (SMA/SMV) to evaluate for ischemia given the embolic risk profile (mechanical valves, subtherapeutic INR, splenic infarction, digital necrosis).

grounding: History: CT abdomen showed no acute intra-abdominal process, unchanged splenic infarction, pancreatic stone with post ductal dilation
History: 2 week history of hypoperfusion to right middle finger, resulting of necrosis distal to the DIP
History: INR was most recently low at 1.9, with adjustment in his coumadin dose
History: 2 mechanical valves (prosthetic aortic and mitral valves)
History: recurrent small bowel obstructions with an ostomy in place
History: ESRD on HD, CAD, Diastolic CHF
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has a massively elevated lipase (2017 IU/L) with a pancreatic stone and post-ductal dilation on the prior non-contrast CT, making pancreatitis the leading diagnosis, but the severity — specifically whether there is pancreatic necrosis — cannot be determined without IV contrast. Simultaneously, the constellation of subtherapeutic INR on mechanical valves, splenic infarction, and digital necrosis raises serious concern for ongoing thromboembolic disease that could involve the mesenteric vasculature, which would be life-threatening and requires contrast-enhanced imaging to evaluate. A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is therefore ordered to simultaneously grade pancreatitis severity, assess the biliary tree, and evaluate mesenteric vessel patency.

vindication.actual_finding: Contrast-enhanced CT showed homogeneous hepatic enhancement with no biliary ductal dilation, a pancreatic body calcification with post-obstructive ductal dilation to 5 mm, patent portal/splenic/superior mesenteric veins, dense calcifications at the celiac and SMA origins (with the SMA likely filling retrograde), no pancreatic parenchymal necrosis, and an unchanged splenic infarction — with no bowel wall thickening or ischemia.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The contrast study addressed all three expected domains: pancreatic parenchymal enhancement was preserved (no necrotizing pancreatitis), biliary anatomy was characterized (no choledocholithiasis or biliary dilation to explain the stone/lipase elevation), and mesenteric vascular patency was assessed (SMV/portal vein patent, but severe SMA calcification with retrograde filling raises concern for chronic mesenteric ischemia rather than acute embolic occlusion).
